title: Moira Vs. Town Council

jokes.text: Look at the state of this place, it's like we're in a Mumbai-an slum.
jokes.setup: Moira is lamenting the decline in their living standards.
jokes.punchline: She compares their modest motel existence to a 'Mumbai-an slum' — an absurdly hyperbolic and culturally tone-deaf comparison that perfectly encapsulates her oblivious elitism.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely rooted in Moira's character — only she would deploy this kind of obliviously grandiose, culturally clueless comparison. The neologism 'Mumbai-an' adds an extra layer of comic pomposity. Strong character integration lifts what could be a simple exaggeration into something specific and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We've been reduced to living like we're in a Mumbai slum. A Mum-BYE slum, if you will.

jokes.text: A year ago we had a staff of twenty-five working the grounds of our home, and now we walk by a mattress on the side of the road, without thinking twice about it.
jokes.setup: Moira is cataloguing their fall from grace.
jokes.punchline: The detail that they now walk past a roadside mattress 'without thinking twice' reveals how thoroughly they've been normalized to poverty — which is itself the indignity she's mourning.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the self-indicting detail: the very thing she's complaining about (normalizing poverty) she's already done. Good layered irony, though it functions more as a scene-setter than a standalone punch.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I simply cannot live like this. The squalor, the filth, the absolute degradation of it all.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, we've been through this. We're doing the best we can with what we have.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, at least that broken toilet is blocking the view of the broken sink.

jokes.text: the good news is that mattress is covering up the cracked toilet seat.
jokes.setup: Moira is lamenting the roadside mattress as a symbol of their degraded circumstances.
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals the mattress is actually serving a practical purpose — covering a broken toilet — which is even more degrading and completely undercuts Moira's dignity argument.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical. Johnny's deadpan reveal doesn't just get a laugh — it deepens the joke by showing their situation is even worse than Moira acknowledged. The structure is airtight: her complaint becomes his setup, and the punchline escalates the misery while deflating her speech. Strong earned humor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: As Oprah once said, 'You get a car, and you get a car, and everybody gets a car!'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I think it was more like, 'Believe in yourself and you can achieve anything.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Or was that... someone famous? A celebrity of some kind?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It could've been from a movie. Or possibly a fortune cookie.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The point is, someone important said something inspirational once, and it changed my life.

jokes.text: Goldie Hawn quote: 'You are the life you accept for yourself.' — 'Those are Goldie's words. Or something someone said to her in India. Or perhaps she read it.'
jokes.setup: Moira invokes a profound quote attributed to Goldie Hawn to inspire Johnny.
jokes.punchline: She immediately walks back the attribution — 'or something someone said to her in India. Or perhaps she read it' — demolishing the gravitas of her own inspirational speech.
jokes.explanation: Moira deflating her own inspirational moment is quintessential Schitt's Creek character comedy. The escalating hedges ('or something someone said to her… or perhaps she read it') are perfectly structured and feel natural to Moira's verbose, oblivious persona. The joke lands on character authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, someone needs to do something about the aesthetic of this town. It's simply unacceptable. The storefronts are dreary, the streetlamps are pedestrian, and don't even get me started on the color palette of the buildings. Surely the town council has a budget for this sort of thing?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you can't just expect the town to redesign itself because you don't like it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not going to do it, David. I didn't move to this godforsaken place to work.

jokes.text: 'Would it kill someone to plant a few peonies?!' — 'I have a feeling if you want peonies, Moira, you're gonna have to plant them yourself.' — 'These are dark times John, but not that dark.'
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests Moira plant peonies herself if she wants them.
jokes.punchline: Moira treats the idea of doing manual labor as equivalent to the darkest possible fate, delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. 'These are dark times John, but not that dark' is a perfectly calibrated line — simultaneously characterizing Moira's bottomless entitlement, landing as a crisp punchline, and being eminently quotable. The structure is clean: suggestion → absolute refusal framed as existential commentary. High character integration; this could only be Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need someone to plant peonies.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You could plant them yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Myself? Johnny, I have a very specific relationship with dirt, and it's not a planting one.

jokes.text: I love how we can just be silent with each other.
jokes.setup: A beat of silence between Alexis and Mutt.
jokes.punchline: She immediately starts talking about how nice it is to not talk — the first ironic contradiction that opens the running argument.
jokes.explanation: A solid ironic setup that initiates the Alexis/Mutt 'non-fight fight.' The joke is more structural — it sets up the escalating argument — than it is a devastating standalone punch. Competent and character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I am not planting flowers. I don't care if we're living in a barn and eating gruel. There are some lines that, even in our current state of desperation, I will not cross. And manual labor is one of them.

jokes.text: You're talking about how nice it is not to talk, but you're still, talking about it. — 'I mean, yeah, I'm not a mute!'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been talking at length about the beauty of silence.
jokes.punchline: Mutt points out the contradiction; Alexis's response ('I'm not a mute!') is completely missing the point, which IS the point.
jokes.explanation: The irony is well-constructed and Alexis's defensive non-sequitur is funny. The comedy is in how thoroughly she refuses to acknowledge the contradiction. Escalates the scene effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know what's nice? This. Just... the silence. It's so peaceful.

jokes.text: 'Well, that is an understatement.' — 'Are we having some non-fight fight or something?'
jokes.setup: Alexis insists 'I'm not a mute!'
jokes.punchline: Mutt's dry 'that is an understatement' is the funnier beat; Alexis naming the dynamic ('non-fight fight') while actively being in one adds a meta layer.
jokes.explanation: Two quick jokes layered together. Mutt's understatement is sharp and economical. Alexis coining 'non-fight fight' while being in one is a nice self-aware bit. Both work, neither is exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just really love silence, you know? Like, I could just sit in silence for hours. It's so peaceful and zen and...

jokes.text: Are those my mannequins? — Yeah. — Well, where are they going?! — Oh, I sold them on eBay.
jokes.setup: Stevie notices her mannequins being carted out of the store.
jokes.punchline: David casually reveals he sold them on eBay — treating someone else's property as his inventory without a second thought.
jokes.explanation: David's blithe appropriation of Stevie's property is pure character comedy — his total lack of guilt is the joke. The beat lands cleanly and sets up the subsequent store-economics argument.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The beauty of silence is really something special. It's so peaceful and serene. I mean, when you really think about it, silence is like the ultimate luxury. Most people don't appreciate it enough.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You've been talking for twenty minutes.

jokes.text: I'm using the money to buy other mannequins, ones that are a little less um... Fun? — Porn-y.
jokes.setup: David is explaining his mannequin replacement plan.
jokes.punchline: Stevie guesses 'fun' but David corrects her: 'porn-y' — a casual, specific, and unexpected descriptor for Stevie's taste in mannequins.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection from 'fun' to 'porn-y' is a tight, well-executed beat. Economy is excellent — one unexpected word does a lot of work in characterizing both David's aesthetic judgment and Stevie's apparently odd tastes. Clean and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not a mute.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: No, you're the opposite of a mute.

jokes.text: Alexander Wang once fired my friend over the smell of his cologne. To Alex's credit, 'Curve Pour Hommes' hasn't been the look since '97.
jokes.setup: David is explaining that scent is crucial to defining a brand.
jokes.punchline: The anecdote about Alexander Wang firing someone for cologne grounds the fashion-world pretension; the specific detail about 'Curve Pour Hommes' being dated since '97 is precise and confident in a way that's funny.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because of David's total seriousness and the specificity of the reference. Only David would casually be on first-name terms with 'Alex' Wang while also having a definitive opinion on the expiration date of a mid-tier cologne. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I have to keep talking because if I stop, no one else will say anything, and then it'll just be silent, and I'll die.

jokes.text: Yeah, I used that, too. [re: company credit card]
jokes.setup: Stevie tells David he can use the company credit card, implying he hasn't yet.
jokes.punchline: David casually reveals he already used it, treating the obvious as an afterthought.
jokes.explanation: Short, punchy, character-consistent. David's breezy admission that he'd already run up the card is delivered with perfect nonchalance. Economical and effective if not memorable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're fun.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: They look pornographic.

jokes.text: No jewelry stands. — No jewelry... — Noooo! — No, David! Jewelry stands!
jokes.setup: David is vetoing Stevie's ideas for the store; he rejects 'jewelry stands.'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's escalating 'Noooo!' followed by the exasperated correction — the comedy is in how much David's single veto devastates her, and then how quickly she pivots to insisting.
jokes.explanation: A light physical-vocal comedy moment. The 'Noooo!' is more of a performance beat than a crafted joke, but it lands as a fun escalation in the store argument. Functional rather than exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Have you seen my scarves? I want to pair them with some of my knick-knacks.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I sold those.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah, they were taking up space in the store.

jokes.text: There is a filthy mattress just lying on the side of the road — 'Oh no, that's Dick Sinson's mattress, he's giving it away.'
jokes.setup: Moira uses the mattress as Exhibit A in her indictment of the town's decay.
jokes.punchline: Ray calmly identifies it as a specific neighbor's mattress being generously given away — a perfectly mundane small-town explanation that obliterates Moira's dramatic framing.
jokes.explanation: This is a classic callback-and-deflation structure done well. Moira has built the mattress into a symbol of civilizational decline; Ray's matter-of-fact local knowledge ('Dick Sinson's mattress, he's giving it away') completely punctures her pretension. The specificity of the name 'Dick Sinson' adds an extra comic texture. Strong craft.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: When I was working in fashion, there was this designer who insisted on wearing the same cologne every single day. He said it was part of his brand identity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: One day, someone asked him what it was, and he said, 'It's a custom blend that costs $300 a bottle.'

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Turns out it was just Old Spice and regret.

jokes.text: I have always seen you as a sensitive, cultured, man of the world. You have that striking accent. — [beat] — Winnipeg.
jokes.setup: Moira flatters Ray by implying he's a cosmopolitan émigré, building up to asking where his family immigrated from.
jokes.punchline: Winnipeg — not the exotic origin the setup implies, but a Canadian prairie city. The setup's romantic framing makes the completely domestic answer hilarious.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is textbook misdirection: Moira's elaborate compliment about a 'striking accent' and 'family who immigrated' strongly implies an exotic backstory. 'Winnipeg' is the perfect one-word demolition — not internationally exciting, just another Canadian city. The double beat of Ray saying it and then Moira repeating it flatly adds one more layer. Economical, character-driven, and earns a genuine laugh.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I got some jewelry stands, a few decorative boxes, and some throw pillows.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, you got jewelry stands? Like, stands for jewelry?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Alexis. Stands. For jewelry.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But... why would jewelry need to stand? Jewelry doesn't stand. Jewelry is small.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a display piece.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So you're displaying... the stand?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You display jewelry *on* the stand, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. OH MY GOD. You're making furniture... for jewelry. That's insane.

jokes.text: I resigned a few weeks ago... And council wasn't too happy about it... I'm surprised you didn't hear, I talked about it extensively on my podcast.
jokes.setup: Moira pitches Ray to help her get her complaint to council, having been told he'd be 'easiest to crack.'
jokes.punchline: Ray has already resigned from council and references his podcast as a major communications channel — the absurdity of a small-town real estate agent having a podcast that he considers must-listen content.
jokes.explanation: Ray's podcast is a quietly absurd running detail about his self-importance. 'I talked about it extensively on my podcast' is the kind of specific small-town delusion that Schitt's Creek does well. Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your accent is so striking. Where are you from originally?

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Winnipeg.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh. I was thinking somewhere more... exotic.

jokes.text: 'I'm surprised you didn't hear, I talked about it extensively on my podcast.' — 'I don't know how I missed that.'
jokes.setup: Ray treats his podcast as a significant local news source.
jokes.punchline: Moira's dry 'I don't know how I missed that' — pure sarcasm delivered with perfect Moira detachment.
jokes.explanation: Moira's sarcasm is so dry it almost reads as sincere, which is exactly right. The economy is excellent — six words do a lot of comedic work in simultaneously mocking Ray and staying true to Moira's imperious register.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ray, I need your help with a complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Of course, Moira. What seems to be the problem?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was told you'd be a pushover.

jokes.text: Look at us, son, a couple of working stiffs back from the salt mines. — What?! — Two men, back from the mines. — What mines? What's that mean?
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to bond with David over a shared blue-collar idiom.
jokes.punchline: David has absolutely no idea what 'salt mines' means and requires a full explanation, exposing the generational and experiential gap — and also the absurdity of Johnny adopting working-class vernacular.
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes from two directions: Johnny awkwardly trying on working-class solidarity, and David's complete bafflement. The escalating explanation ('when miners worked in the, salt industry, they'd come home, after digging... salt') as Johnny tries to explain it to himself is beautifully awkward. Strong character integration for both.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: I'm not a pushover. I'm simply a person who enjoys saying yes to things.

jokes.text: When miners worked in the, salt industry, they'd come home, after digging... salt.
jokes.setup: David says he doesn't know what 'back from the salt mines' means.
jokes.punchline: John's attempt to explain it devolves into a hilariously circular non-explanation — 'they'd come home, after digging... salt' — the pause before 'salt' acknowledging he has nothing more to offer.
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'salt' is everything — it signals John's total defeat in the face of basic etymology. His circular explanation ('they mined salt... they dug... salt') is perfectly structured as a false summit, and Eugene Levy's delivery would be impeccable. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: I'd be happy to help you with the council.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ray, you're not on the council.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Well, no, but I have a podcast.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Does anyone listen to it?

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: My mother downloads every episode.

jokes.text: You know I just got back from a four-hour shift that mostly consisted of me sampling luxury candles so...
jokes.setup: Johnny has sympathetically said 'that's a tough day' re: David's work.
jokes.punchline: David deadpans that his hardship was four hours of luxury candle sampling — the gap between the gravitas of 'tough day' and the cushy reality is the joke.
jokes.explanation: David playing his candle-sampling as genuine hardship while both he and the audience know it's ridiculous is perfectly calibrated. The trailing 'so...' is doing extra comedic work — implying a level of suffering that requires no further elaboration. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, David, sometimes you just have to go out there and give it the old college try.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, I went to college. I don't remember anyone trying anything 'old' about it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's just an expression, son.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's a very confusing one. And frankly, it makes you sound like you're about to tell me about the Depression.

jokes.text: Why don't you grab yourself a cold one? You've earned it. Working man. — There's nothing in here. — Really? — Yeah. — Oh, I must've taken the last one.
jokes.setup: Johnny grandly tells David to grab a cold beer as reward for his hard work.
jokes.punchline: Johnny drank the last one himself, collapsing his blue-collar camaraderie performance instantly.
jokes.explanation: A quick, satisfying deflation gag. Johnny builds up the moment and then immediately undercuts it. 'Oh, I must've taken the last one' delivered casually is the right tone. Clean structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you know, when someone says they're going to the salt mines, it's just an expression. It means you're going to work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But why salt? Why not just say you're going to work?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Because... it's an expression. People say it. It's a figure of speech.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but what is the origin? Why salt mines specifically?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I... it's just something people say when they're talking about, you know, going to work. Hard work. Difficult labor.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you don't actually know.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No. I have no idea.

jokes.text: I mean, you got this job all on your own, nobody had to make a call, nobody got involved, nobody had to... pay somebody off.
jokes.setup: Johnny is sincerely (it seems) praising David for getting a job on his own merits.
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of things that DIDN'T happen ('make a call… pay somebody off') implies a long history of exactly those things happening — and the pause before 'pay somebody off' is a perfect beat.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed joke that reveals character backstory through negation. By listing all the corrupt things that DIDN'T happen, Johnny inadvertently confesses to a lifetime of nepotism and bribery. The escalation to 'pay somebody off' with a tiny hesitation is the comic peak. Strong originality in its approach and very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not really a 'working man.' I organized color swatches by tone and opacity.

jokes.text: And you're looking very, very sharp. — Well... — Is that a new sweater? — Uh, it is. — Well, word to the wise, son, don't... spend all your money at once.
jokes.setup: Johnny pivots from praise to backhanded parenting.
jokes.punchline: He admires David's sweater and then immediately uses it as an excuse to lecture about spending — the compliment and the criticism are inseparably tangled.
jokes.explanation: Solid father-son character comedy. The pivot from compliment to criticism is natural and recognizable. Doesn't reach the heights of the 'nobody had to pay somebody off' beat but it's a warm, functional continuation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Here, have a beer. You've earned it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh... I drank the last one.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Of course you did.

jokes.text: Word to the wise. When you're... speaking to somebody wise, you lis... you're cognizant of what they're saying. A wise person telling you... Anyway, just-just uh, keep that in mind.
jokes.setup: David asks what 'word to the wise' even means.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's circular, self-undermining non-definition — he can't actually explain it and trails off into 'Anyway' — is the joke. He's supposed to be the wise one.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's fumbling attempt to explain an idiom he uses with authority is a nice character moment. The comedy is in the collapse — he starts confidently and ends with 'Anyway.' Mirrors the 'salt mines' bit earlier in tone.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm very proud of you, David. You got this job entirely on your own merit.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, usually I have to make a few calls, smooth things over with the manager, maybe take them to lunch.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Sometimes slip them an envelope under the table. Once, I may have promised a man a timeshare in Boca Raton. But this time? All you.

jokes.text: Oh my God! Your face! — Yes, you can see it now! — You shaved!
jokes.setup: Alexis wakes up (seemingly from a nap) to find Mutt has shaved his beard.
jokes.punchline: Her panicked 'Oh my God! Your face!' as if something is wrong with it — the horror-movie register applied to a routine grooming decision.
jokes.explanation: A light physical-reveal comedy moment. Funny in the moment because of the performance, but the joke is fairly simple. Sets up the more developed argument that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I promise I won't interfere with your life anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But you really should diversify your portfolio. You're putting too much into the store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you're interfering.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, I'm not. I'm just saying, as someone who's lost everything, I know a thing or two about financial ruin.

jokes.text: And you didn't talk to me about it. / About me shaving my beard? / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Alexis is upset Mutt didn't consult her before shaving.
jokes.punchline: That Alexis expects to be consulted on a partner's personal grooming decision is both comic and revealing — connecting back to the episode's communication theme.
jokes.explanation: Good character reveal — Alexis's need for input extends to areas that objectively aren't her domain. The absurdity of the boundary she's drawing is well-conveyed in Mutt's incredulous echo.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What does that even mean?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you know, it's just an expression. When something is... it's like when you're in a situation and things are... they're not going the way you thought they would.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not an explanation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, I know what I'm doing. It means like, uh, when you assume something is going to happen a certain way, but then it doesn't, so you're caught off guard by the... the unexpectedness of it all.

jokes.text: 'hm, no mutt, I don't think that that's the right journey for you at this point in time.' — 'Well, then I would've said, too bad.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is role-playing how the conversation should have gone if Mutt had consulted her about shaving.
jokes.punchline: Her scripted version involves a solemn verdict about his 'journey'; his scripted counter is simply 'too bad.' The gap between her elaborate emotional process and his total indifference is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's language ('the right journey for you at this point in time') is perfectly self-parodying — wellness-speak applied to a shaving decision. Mutt's blunt 'too bad' is the right comic counterweight. Strong character integration for Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, someone's breaking in! No, wait... they're taking things. They're robbing us! We're being robbed! This is so typical. First we lose all our money, and now someone's stealing what's left. I literally cannot afford to be robbed right now. Do you know how expensive it is to replace stolen items when you're poor? I don't even have insurance anymore. Well, I have insurance, but it's not the kind that covers robbery. It's the kind that covers, like, being too fabulous for regular coverage or whatever.

jokes.text: I was a beard for a very well known actor, and I get it, it's uncomfortable.
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to relate to beard discomfort.
jokes.punchline: She casually reveals she was a 'beard' (romantic cover) for a closeted celebrity — treating this as a mundane point of comparison for facial hair discomfort.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The wordplay on 'beard' (facial hair vs. cover relationship) is deployed with such casual confidence that the double meaning only lands a beat late — Alexis isn't making a pun, she's sharing a personal anecdote. The joke only works because it's Alexis: it's completely plausible given her backstory and reveals character while getting a huge laugh. High originality, high economy, excellent character integration. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no. No, no, no. What is happening right now? Where did you go? Like, where's the beard? That was like, your whole thing. That was your identity. I don't even know who you are right now. You look like a completely different person. Like, a person I would never date. Which is good because I'm already dating you, so that would be weird, but also deeply concerning because I'm not attracted to this.

jokes.text: But your beard is like, my favorite thing about you! — My beard is your favorite thing about me! — I said one of my favorite things. — No, no, no, no. You said, 'favorite thing!'
jokes.setup: Alexis slips and calls Mutt's beard her 'favorite thing' about him.
jokes.punchline: Mutt catches the unintended slight and won't let it go; Alexis's backtrack only makes it worse. The argument is about a shave but suddenly about the entire relationship's depth.
jokes.explanation: A recognizable relationship dynamic played with good energy. The comedy is in the trap Alexis walks herself into and can't get out of. The escalation is well-managed, but it's a familiar comedic structure.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, okay, so David and I have been trying to communicate better, right? And I was like, 'babe, we need to go on this journey together.' And he was like, 'what journey?' And I'm like, 'the journey of like, deciding whether you're gonna shave or not.'

jokes.text: Look at your little doll lips.
jokes.setup: After a charged argument about the beard, the conversation has apparently wound down.
jokes.punchline: Alexis — who moments ago was furious about the beard — is now charmed by his bare face, calling his lips 'little doll lips.' The emotional whiplash and the specific, slightly condescending phrase is the comedy.
jokes.explanation: A single-line punchline that resolves the entire beard argument in the most characteristically Alexis way possible: through shallow aesthetic appreciation. The whiplash from emotional grievance to 'little doll lips' is perfectly timed and uniquely her voice. High economy — four words do everything. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was a beard for Chad Mulligan for like six months. He's a closeted actor.

jokes.text: Thank you for installing the ramp at the post office. But it's a little steep. I've tried it twice, and both times, I've been sort of shot back into traffic.
jokes.setup: A citizen thanks the council for an accessibility ramp.
jokes.punchline: The ramp is so steep it shoots wheelchair users into traffic — a well-meaning accessibility improvement that's actually dangerous. The understatement 'sort of shot back into traffic' is the comedic register.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed background joke that captures small-town bureaucratic incompetence. The politeness of 'thank you for installing the ramp' followed by 'it's been shooting me into traffic' is a perfect dark-comic deflation. Good structure, though it's from a minor character without much integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Wait, so your favorite thing about me was my beard?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, I didn't say that—

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You literally just said it was your favorite thing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, it was *a* favorite thing. You have many favorite things about you.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Name one.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Your... your passion for the environment.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You once asked me what a watershed was.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I like that you're... outdoorsy.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: You got poison oak that one time we went hiking and you complained the entire way back to the car.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but I like your beard, Mutt. I really do.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Now you're just saying that.

jokes.text: I was just hoping to be interrupted by a concerned citizen.
jokes.setup: Moira barges into the council meeting mid-discussion and announces she has a concern.
jokes.punchline: Roland's flat sarcasm — 'I was just hoping to be interrupted by a concerned citizen' — nails the weary small-town official voice.
jokes.explanation: Roland's dry line is well-calibrated. It's sarcastic without being mean-spirited, and it accurately characterizes his resigned relationship with Moira. Economical and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you have lips. Like, full lips. They're very... pouty. It's like you've been hiding a whole other face under there.

jokes.text: Bob, just concentrate on the minutes, okay? I can tell right now you're behind. — Okay, typing is not my strong suit. — Ray could type without looking at the keys.
jokes.setup: The council is trying to get Moira to wait for the agenda; Roland tells Bob to keep up with the minutes.
jokes.punchline: Bob admits typing isn't his strong suit; Moira immediately inserts a gratuitous comparison to Ray (who resigned). It's petty, specific, and perfectly her.
jokes.explanation: Moira's casually devastating comparison ('Ray could type without looking at the keys') is wonderfully petty and small. It's a throwaway line that perfectly captures her oblivious one-upmanship. Character integration is the main strength here.

dialogue.speaker: Jazzagal (Ronnie)
dialogue.line: The ramp launches wheelchair users backward into traffic

jokes.text: Bob, just concentrate on the minutes, okay? I can tell right now you're behind. Okay, typing is not my strong suit. Ray could type without looking at the keys.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to address the council; Roland is managing his secretary Bob.
jokes.punchline: Moira's aside — 'Ray could type without looking at the keys' — is a hilariously specific and petty dig at Bob's typing, delivered as if touch-typing is an impressive accomplishment worthy of note.
jokes.explanation: Moira name-dropping the resigned Ray as a superior touch-typist is a nice small joke. The specificity of 'without looking at the keys' is funnier than a general compliment would be.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh good, Moira's here. Now we can finally get this meeting started.

jokes.text: Well, actually, Jennifer had the floor. / Hello.
jokes.setup: Moira is steamrolling the council meeting, ignoring procedure.
jokes.punchline: Jennifer's mild 'Hello' — a reminder that she still exists and still hasn't finished her accessibility complaint — is a perfect deadpan beat.
jokes.explanation: One word that does everything. Jennifer waving from the sidelines while being completely ignored is quiet comedic gold. The economy score is near-perfect — you literally can't get more efficient than 'Hello.'

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This toilet paper is like party streamers. It's thin, it's flimsy, and it falls apart the moment you actually use it.

jokes.text: David, if you're going out again, can you pick up some new toilet paper? I cannot use the stuff the motel provides, okay? It's like a birthday streamer. You-you-it stretches, and then, and then you pull it, and it just snaps, okay? That-that whole thing just fell off the roller!
jokes.setup: Johnny calls out to David for a mundane errand request.
jokes.punchline: He delivers an escalating, increasingly specific and intimate rant about the motel toilet paper — including the simulation of it snapping and 'falling off the roller.' The 'birthday streamer' simile is the comedic peak.
jokes.explanation: The 'birthday streamer' simile is inspired — it's vivid and unexpected. Johnny's agitated, escalating delivery of a toilet paper complaint while apparently unaware (or unconcerned) that Stevie is present is great situational comedy. The rant has good energy even if it runs slightly long.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You heard that?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Every word.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was... I was trying to be funny.

jokes.text: I know Stevie was here. That's why I came in with something funny.
jokes.setup: David reminds Johnny that Stevie is present; Johnny seems embarrassed.
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals the toilet paper rant was intentional performance comedy for Stevie's benefit — he came in 'with something funny.' The self-awareness is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A reframing joke that retroactively makes the previous bit funnier. By revealing the rant was a deliberate comedic performance, it gives Johnny unexpected self-awareness and adds a meta layer. The callback structure earns the rewatch bonus.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not going anywhere. This is better than cable.

jokes.text: What's this? Looks expensive. Oh, this is some new bedding. David, didn't I just... Tell you to save your money?
jokes.setup: John has just told David to save his money; he then immediately notices new bedding in David's room.
jokes.punchline: His resigned 'Didn't I just... Tell you to save your money?' after the dots of recognition — the pause suggesting the futility of ever having said it.
jokes.explanation: Functional callback but predictable — the audience already knows David spent money, so John discovering it is the expected beat rather than a surprise. Works as setup for the extended write-off argument rather than as a standalone.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A write-off is when you write off a bad debt.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Right, so you just write it off.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it's not quite that simple.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: But you do write it off though?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, but—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that whenever I have a problem, I can just make it go away by writing it off?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, that's not what a write-off is.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Then what is it? You just... write it off!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's for tax purposes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, so it IS free money from the government!

jokes.text: I was gonna leave, but now I don't want to.
jokes.setup: Johnny has begun lecturing David about the new bedding.
jokes.punchline: Stevie, who was about to politely exit a family moment, decides to stay for the entertainment — delivered with complete deadpan.
jokes.explanation: Seven words of perfect Stevie. Her role as dry audience-surrogate is at its best here — she's voicing exactly what the viewing audience feels. Maximum economy, strong character integration, very quotable in context.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So who actually pays for these write-offs?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's a deduction, so... the government?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The government pays for your write-offs?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I mean... you write off the expense, so you don't pay taxes on it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So the government loses money.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, technically, everyone loses a little bit of money.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Including you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I write it off. That's the whole thing. You write off people.

jokes.text: Do you even know what a write off is?! — Uh yeah, it's when you buy something for your business and the government pays you back for it. — Oh, and who pays for it? — Nobody, you write it off. — Who writes it off?! — I don't know, the govern... The 'write off' people!
jokes.setup: Johnny challenges David on his understanding of 'write offs.'
jokes.punchline: David's increasingly desperate non-explanation — culminating in 'the write off people' — reveals total financial ignorance delivered with complete confidence.
jokes.explanation: A standout comedic sequence. David's escalating explanations are perfectly written — each answer is slightly more absurd than the last, but delivered with total confidence, culminating in the beautiful 'the write off people!' The joke works on multiple levels: it characterizes David's privileged ignorance, captures a real confusion about taxation, and escalates beautifully. 'The write off people' is among the most quotable lines in the episode. Very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My complexion is a direct reflection of the Rose Apothecary brand. If my skin looks bad, the store looks bad. Therefore, this $300 moisturizer is a business expense.

jokes.text: Oh, and who pays for it? Nobody, you write it off. Who writes it off?! I don't know, the govern... The 'write off' people!
jokes.setup: John is trying to correct David's understanding of write-offs.
jokes.punchline: 'The write off people!' — David's last-ditch invention of an imaginary government department responsible for write-offs is the absurdist peak of his financial ignorance.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The escalating Socratic exchange — 'who pays for it? / nobody / who writes it off?!' — is perfectly structured, and 'The write off people!' is the ideal absurdist peak. It's quotable, character-specific, and reveals David's profound financial naivety in the funniest possible way. Earns its 8+ — comparable in construction to the calibration anchors.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to return some things.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I bought a massage chair.

jokes.text: But the skin care products you got this morning, those are a write off. What skin care products? You purchased skin care products?!
jokes.setup: David is demonstrating his write-off knowledge by applying it to John's purchases.
jokes.punchline: David casually reveals John bought skin care products this morning — apparently a secret — triggering outrage from John at being caught, not at David's tax advice.
jokes.explanation: Good reversal — David catches John in the same kind of purchase he was being criticized for. The hypocrisy reveal escalates the argument nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, I have standards. I'm not going to lower them just because we're living in a motel. It's like... it's like having a baby in prison. You don't just put the toilet in the nursery.

jokes.text: Okay, well then why isn't it called a 'tax write off?'
jokes.setup: Johnny explains that a write off is a business expense to reduce taxable income.
jokes.punchline: David's response — 'why isn't it called a tax write off?' — is both stupid and somehow reasonable-sounding, which is exactly the right register.
jokes.explanation: The perfect capstone to the write-off argument. The joke is that David's question is idiotic but delivered with such genuine puzzlement that it almost sounds logical. Johnny's exasperated 'It is!' is the implicit punchline. Economical, earned, and very quotable. The fact that it lands as a separate beat from the earlier escalation shows good structural awareness from the writers.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: When you carry something for nine months, you develop a certain attachment to it. It's like a prison baby.

dialogue.speaker: Council member
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, a prison baby?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, you know, when you're incarcerated and you have a baby in prison.

dialogue.speaker: Council member
dialogue.line: I... I don't think that's a real thing.

jokes.text: Well then, I'll return some things. There's not enough space in here for the massage chair, anyway.
jokes.setup: Johnny has just won the argument about write-offs and told David he can't buy things for himself.
jokes.punchline: David casually reveals there's ALSO a massage chair on order — a new item we haven't heard about, raising the stakes of his spending.
jokes.explanation: A good escalation tag. Just when it seems the argument is resolved, David casually mentions a massage chair — widening the scope of his spending. The throwaway delivery is right; it's almost an afterthought, which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not saying I have a prison baby, I'm saying my relationship with my eyebrows is like a prison baby — I didn't ask for it, I didn't plan for it, and now I'm stuck with it.

jokes.text: I am here to address an issue that I believe — Self-respect.
jokes.setup: Moira has gathered the council's attention and is about to launch into her prepared speech.
jokes.punchline: She pauses on 'I believe' and then delivers a single word: 'Self-respect.' — the topic is both profound and absurdly self-regarding.
jokes.explanation: Moira's dramatic pause and single-word topic ('self-respect') is a good performance beat. The comedy is in the grandiosity of her delivery relative to a municipal complaint about litter. Character-specific and well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Council member
dialogue.line: So just to clarify... how many prison babies are we talking about?

jokes.text: I am here to address an issue that I believe... Self-respect.
jokes.setup: Moira has taken the floor at council and begins her speech about an issue she believes in.
jokes.punchline: Before she can finish, a council member completes her sentence with 'Self-respect' — and then adds, 'I don't think we can help you in that department.'
jokes.explanation: The council member's pre-emptive 'self-respect' completion and follow-up 'we can't help you there' is a good deadpan putdown. Works because it implies the council already has Moira's number from prior encounters.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: You know, I've noticed you never eat solid food. It's always smoothies and protein shakes and things you can drink through a straw.

jokes.text: I don't think we can help you in that department.
jokes.setup: Moira announces she's there to address 'self-respect.'
jokes.punchline: Roland's flat response — they can't help her with self-respect — is both a dig at Moira and at the council's own limits.
jokes.explanation: A tight, quick Roland burn. The ambiguity — is he saying they can't help with self-respect in general, or that Moira specifically needs it? — gives it an extra edge. Very economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I think we need to work on our communication. Like, when you say something, I don't always understand what you mean.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Okay, what do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, like, you use a lot of words that I don't think are real words. Or you use them in a way that doesn't make sense to me.

jokes.text: A baby born in a prison cell does not know that a toilet should not be in the same room as its bed, but we do.
jokes.setup: Moira is building a rhetorical argument for why the town should have higher standards.
jokes.punchline: Her analogy — a prison baby who doesn't know toilets and beds shouldn't share a room — is both earnest and completely bizarre as civic rhetoric.
jokes.explanation: Moira's 'prison baby' analogy is genuinely inspired absurdist rhetoric. She's trying to make a point about aspiration and dignity, but the image she chooses is so specific and strange that it derails itself. The joke is that she's completely serious. High originality — you don't hear this kind of analogy often — and very high character integration. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about. I am notoriously efficient. Why, just last week, I organized a benefit for the de-clawing of Winnie Mandela's cat.

jokes.text: I didn't know you'd been in prison.
jokes.setup: Moira delivers her prison baby analogy as pure metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Roland takes it completely literally, assuming Moira has a personal prison history — a perfect small-town straight-man response.
jokes.explanation: Five words of perfect comedic deflation. Roland's literal interpretation of Moira's already-absurd metaphor creates a double layer of comedy. It also subtly suggests that a Schitt's Creek council member finding out Moira had been in prison would not be entirely surprising. Excellent economy, strong character integration for Roland.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, your wife, she's like a force of nature. She comes in here, she makes a big production out of everything, but somehow things just... get done. It's like watching a tornado in a wig organize a filing system.

jokes.text: Okay, picture all of you living in a cave, having never seen the outside world... And then I show up.
jokes.setup: After the prison analogy fails, Moira tries a new metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Her cave metaphor is bad enough, but the punchline is 'and then I show up' — positioning herself as the light of civilization emerging to enlighten the townsfolk.
jokes.explanation: Moira's messianic self-insertion into her own metaphor is peak character comedy. She doesn't realize how condescending it sounds because she genuinely believes it. 'And then I show up' is a beautifully concise encapsulation of her worldview. Very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The eczema benefit was a nightmare. The air was so thick with flaking skin cells, it looked like it was snowing indoors. People were scratching themselves raw, leaving little piles of dermis on the furniture. I saw one woman shed so much, I thought she was molting. The smell—my God, the smell of a thousand medicated creams mingling together created this sort of... clinical funk that clung to everything. I had to burn my clothes afterward. Twice.

jokes.text: No, this is not what I'm trying to say. — What are you trying to say? — That we should strive for something that sets us apart from the prison baby.
jokes.setup: Moira's cave analogy has also failed; Roland asks what she's actually trying to say.
jokes.punchline: She returns to the prison baby — 'sets us apart from the prison baby' — as her clearest articulation yet, treating it as though it were a coherent rallying cry.
jokes.explanation: The prison baby callback landing as the actual thesis statement of Moira's speech is excellent escalation and callback work. 'Sets us apart from the prison baby' as a civic motto is absurdly funny and utterly Moira. The joke is that she's doubled down on her worst analogy and made it her best argument. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, you know, it's not just about the relationship ending. It's about all the intimate moments we shared, and how he would just—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, no. I'm not doing this with you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank God.

jokes.text: 'Yeah well, I was actually just gonna suggest that.' — 'Before you suggested it.'
jokes.setup: Moira has demanded greenery, flowers, and boxwoods for the town.
jokes.punchline: Roland claims he was about to suggest it; Moira immediately adds 'before you suggested it' — an unnecessary jab that she can't resist.
jokes.explanation: Moira's reflexive credit-claiming — she can't even let Roland's small diplomatic lie stand — is perfectly in character. The three-word addendum is economical and sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I've never really felt anything after a breakup. I just kind of... move on.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: My therapist says I should probably see a cardiologist about that.

jokes.text: Yeah well, I was actually just gonna suggest that. Before you suggested it.
jokes.setup: Moira has just pushed through her proposal for greenery and a town cleanup.
jokes.punchline: Roland claims he was about to suggest the same thing — and the awkward addition of 'before you suggested it' makes the lie even more transparent.
jokes.explanation: Roland's face-saving 'I was going to suggest that, before you suggested it' is a nice small joke about small-town bureaucratic pride. The 'before you suggested it' clause is the key — it reveals the insincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I need a hug.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

jokes.text: So there was just one baby in prison, or there were more?
jokes.setup: The council meeting has moved forward after Moira's speech.
jokes.punchline: Someone on the council is still thinking about the prison baby and asks for clarification — still taking it literally, as if it were a real anecdote.
jokes.explanation: A delayed callback that functions as a perfect coda to the prison baby running gag. The question is asked with apparent genuine curiosity, extending the joke that the council took the analogy literally. Beautiful economy and timing — it lands as a surprise because the scene seemed to have moved on.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, your sheets are so soft!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're Egyptian cotton, 1000 thread count.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's amazing. You should write those off.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Write off my sheets?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, like a business expense.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, we've been through this. You can't just write off personal items.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But you sleep on them at night, so you're well-rested for work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not how write-offs work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It totally is.

jokes.text: Did you lose weight? — I shaved my beard.
jokes.setup: Twyla greets Mutt and doesn't quite recognize what's different about him.
jokes.punchline: She defaults to the common social faux-pas of asking if someone lost weight; he has simply shaved. Twyla's cheerful obliviousness is her character's comedy signature.
jokes.explanation: A quick, clean gag that fits Twyla's character perfectly. The joke is in her instinctive social response ('did you lose weight?') to something she can't identify. Economical and character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, why is there a condom on the front lawn?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know, David. Perhaps it blew in from the wind?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Or maybe it's from one of the neighbors?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it's on *our* property, so unless you'd like me to go door-to-door with a Ziploc bag, I suggest we just ignore it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'll get it.

jokes.text: Um, what's the cake today? — I'm sorry, did you just ask for cake? I'm not used to you ordering solid food.
jokes.setup: Alexis orders something from the menu.
jokes.punchline: Twyla is shocked that Alexis ordered solid food — implying Alexis essentially never eats real food, only drinks. Twyla's flustered surprise makes it a character revelation joke.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's shock at Alexis eating 'solid food' is a great character-based joke that says a lot about Alexis's typical habits. 'Solid food' is a funny, slightly clinical choice of words that elevates the beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Were these plants always here?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, David. When we first moved in, this place was completely barren. It was like a desert. A wasteland. The walls were bare, the floors were cold and empty. There wasn't a living thing in sight. It was bleaker than my portfolio.

jokes.text: I guess I'm having cake.
jokes.setup: Twyla has pointed out that Alexis doesn't usually order solid food; Mutt has apparently ordered cake for them.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's resigned 'I guess I'm having cake' — accepting an implicit decision that was apparently made without her input — mirrors the beard argument about not being consulted.
jokes.explanation: Small, efficient beat. The resigned acceptance is mildly funny and has a thematic echo of the communication theme, but it's not a strong standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: There's a condom in here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my god, why would you— why would you keep pointing that out?

jokes.text: Hey, next order of business, we install a ramp here.
jokes.setup: After Moira's successful council appearance, she's leaving.
jokes.punchline: Roland's aside — 'next order of business, we install a ramp here' — is a callback to Jennifer's dangerous ramp complaint, suggesting Roland is now committed to more ramp-building, despite the first one nearly killing her.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the ramp joke from the beginning of the council scene. Roland's enthusiasm for installing more ramps — despite the existing one being hazardous — is a nice running-gag element. Economy is good.

jokes.text: You're a pain in the ass, but you get stuff done.
jokes.setup: Roland is acknowledging Moira's effectiveness in getting the council to act quickly.
jokes.punchline: His backhanded compliment is direct and funny — he can't give her a straight compliment, so it comes out as an insult-compliment. Moira's response — 'a delightful compliment' — completes the beat.
jokes.explanation: A tidy character beat that characterizes the Moira-Roland dynamic efficiently. Roland can't help himself; Moira can't help receiving it as pure praise. The economy is the joke's main strength.

jokes.text: Those boxwood trees? — Oh yes, two sets... — I would lower your expectations. You're lucky if you get some daisies in a planter.
jokes.setup: Moira expects her boxwood tree demand to be fulfilled.
jokes.punchline: Roland reality-checks her: 'You're lucky if you get some daisies in a planter.' — the gap between Moira's aspirations and Schitt's Creek reality.
jokes.explanation: Roland's deflation of Moira's ambitions is a classic fish-out-of-water beat. 'Daisies in a planter' as the realistic ceiling of civic ambition is a good specific detail. Functional and character-consistent.

jokes.text: Eczema benefit? — Yes, you try enjoying a puff pastry with everyone around you scratching.
jokes.setup: Roland repeats the name of Moira's charity benefit — 'Artists Against Eczema.'
jokes.punchline: Moira defends the cause with a specific, vivid, and slightly revolting image: trying to eat puff pastry while surrounded by scratching eczema sufferers.
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. 'You try enjoying a puff pastry with everyone around you scratching' is a brilliant, unexpected image — absurdist and specific in the best way. It perfectly captures Moira's world: even her charity work is primarily about her own sensory comfort. The puff pastry detail is inspired. High originality, very strong character integration, very quotable.

jokes.text: What's going on? I think Mutt and I ended things. You think? Okay, here's a serious question: Are you capable of making an executive decision?
jokes.setup: Alexis comes to David saying she 'thinks' she and Mutt ended things.
jokes.punchline: David's 'are you capable of making an executive decision?' skewers Alexis's characteristically provisional, non-committal approach to even her own relationship decisions.
jokes.explanation: David cuts through Alexis's indecision with characteristic bluntness. 'Executive decision' is a nice choice of words — corporate language for a breakup — and the question cuts to the heart of Alexis's season-long character trait.

jokes.text: I think Mutt and I ended things. — You think? Okay, here's a serious question: Are you capable of making an executive decision?
jokes.setup: Alexis announces the end of her relationship with a hedge — 'I think.'
jokes.punchline: David cuts right to what's actually funny — her inability to commit to a decision, framing it as an executive function question.
jokes.explanation: David's instant pivot from emotional support to a critique of Alexis's decision-making is perfectly in character. 'Are you capable of making an executive decision?' is a great line because it's simultaneously unkind and completely accurate.

jokes.text: We both agreed that even though the sex was amazing — Mhmm. — Like, amazing! — [long beat] — There was this time in the barn where I climbed up onto this rafter...
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining the terms of her breakup conversation with Mutt.
jokes.punchline: She goes into graphic specifics about barn sex — while David (her brother) is stuck in the room with no escape, punctuated by the beat before she starts describing the rafter incident.
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy: the pause before 'there was this time in the barn' signals to both David and the audience what's coming. David's forced 'Mhmm' responses to his sister's sex life are perfectly uncomfortable. The barn/rafter detail is specific enough to be genuinely funny. Strong structure and character integration.

jokes.text: What was right in front of you? — When? — Okay.
jokes.setup: Alexis says she and Mutt realized they were holding onto lust rather than 'what was right in front of us.'
jokes.punchline: David asks what was right in front of them; Alexis's baffled 'When?' — as if the question doesn't even compute — and David's immediate 'Okay' (giving up) is a quick, sharp exchange.
jokes.explanation: Three words ('Okay.') as punchline — David cutting his losses rather than pursuing a conversation with Alexis who isn't tracking. Maximum economy, very Schitt's Creek sibling dynamic.

jokes.text: Which was what? What? What was right in front of you? When? Okay. Okay. So anyway, it's done.
jokes.setup: Alexis says she and Mutt realised they were holding onto lust rather than 'what was right in front of them.'
jokes.punchline: David asks what was right in front of them; Alexis doesn't answer; the question gets repeated; Alexis deflects and shuts the conversation down — implying she doesn't actually know.
jokes.explanation: A gentle comedy beat — Alexis's vagueness exposed by David's practical questioning. Works more as an awkward pause than a real joke; its humor is subtle and depends heavily on performance.

jokes.text: Like, I've never felt anything after a break up. Mhmm. — Like, nothing. — Like, I've been to doctors about it.
jokes.setup: Alexis is noting that this breakup feels different because she actually feels something.
jokes.punchline: The dark twist — she's 'been to doctors' about her emotional numbness after breakups — delivered as a casual aside, is unexpectedly bleak and very funny.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. 'I've been to doctors about it' is a genuinely dark revelation about Alexis's emotional history, delivered with complete casualness — which is exactly the right register. It's funny as a joke, but it's also real character depth. The doctors line is unexpected, specific, dark, and utterly Alexis. High originality, very strong character integration. One of the most quotable moments in the episode.

jokes.text: I think I need a hug, or something. — W-you what? — I need a hug. — Uh... uh... — David, will you please give me a hug? — Oh, okay, yes, yeah.
jokes.setup: Alexis, in a rare moment of vulnerability, asks David for a hug.
jokes.punchline: David's panicked, horrified response to the prospect of physical affection from his sister — 'W-you what?' followed by inarticulate stalling — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: David's physical aversion to intimacy — even with family — is a well-established character trait, and this scene pays it off warmly. The comedy and the genuine heart of the scene coexist. His reluctant 'Oh, okay, yes, yeah' is funny and sweet simultaneously. Good character integration and earned emotional payoff.

jokes.text: I think I need a hug, or something. W-you what? I need a hug. Uh... uh... David, will you please give me a hug?
jokes.setup: Alexis has had an emotional moment about the breakup and wants comfort.
jokes.punchline: David's physical discomfort at the prospect of hugging his sister — the stammering 'W-you what? Uh... uh...' — makes their emotionally stunted family dynamic visible and funny.
jokes.explanation: David's inability to process a simple hug request from his own sister reveals the Rose family's catastrophic emotional suppression. The warm punchline — he does eventually hug her — makes it work as both comedy and character development.

jokes.text: Did you get new bedding? — Um, yeah, I did. — But it's a write off, so I don't... Anyway.
jokes.setup: Alexis notices the new bedding during their hug.
jokes.punchline: David immediately starts defending the bedding purchase with the write-off argument — then stops himself — in the middle of a genuinely tender sibling moment.
jokes.explanation: A callback that works beautifully in a tender scene — David's instinct to self-justify the bedding purchase intrudes even into the hug, and his 'Anyway' as he drops it is a perfectly timed character moment. The joke is funnier because of where it lands emotionally.

jokes.text: Did you get new bedding? Um, yeah, I did. But it's a write off, so I don't... Anyway.
jokes.setup: Alexis and David are having their first real sibling moment; Alexis notices the new bedding.
jokes.punchline: David immediately reverts to defending his write-off logic — and then catches himself and drops it — 'Anyway' — allowing the emotional moment to reassert itself.
jokes.explanation: A sweet callback that also reveals David's reflexive defensiveness. The 'Anyway' abandonment is perfectly timed — he knows this isn't the moment, and the truncated joke makes him more human. Works as both comedy and as a warm character moment.

jokes.text: Oh, did you pick up that random condom that was on the grass there? — Ew yeah, whose was that? — I have no idea.
jokes.setup: Johnny is calling the kids out to see Moira's achievement (the boxwoods).
jokes.punchline: David immediately asks if anyone picked up the mysterious condom from the grass — the mundane grossness of motel living intruding on Moira's triumph. Johnny's baffled 'I have no idea' about whose condom it is is an extra beat.
jokes.explanation: A solid gross-out deflation of the family moment. The condom as background motel-life detail is the right kind of mundane indignity for this show. Functions well as a scene-setter.

jokes.text: Oh, did you pick up that random condom that was on the grass there? Ew yeah, whose was that? I have no idea.
jokes.setup: John is proudly showing the family Moira's civic achievement — the new boxwood trees.
jokes.punchline: David asks about the random condom on the grass — a gross unexpected detail — which completely hijacks the proud moment.
jokes.explanation: A mild gross-out joke. The condom detail undercuts Moira's civic victory nicely, but 'random condom on the grass' is more of a setup for the episode's final beat than a strong joke in itself.

jokes.text: Weren't those here before? — There was literally nothing here. — It was a wasteland. — Weren't there more plants here before? — No, there weren't!
jokes.setup: Moira presents her boxwood tree achievement with pride.
jokes.punchline: David and Alexis casually undercut it by suggesting the plants were already there — with Moira and Johnny forced to deny this increasingly emphatically.
jokes.explanation: The kids' total failure to be impressed by Moira's civic triumph is a great family dynamic joke. Their casual 'weren't those here before?' is exactly the right kind of ungrateful indifference. Moira's escalating insistence ('It was a wasteland!') is funny because it's so disproportionate.

jokes.text: Weren't those here before? There was literally nothing here. It was a wasteland. Weren't there more plants here before? No, there weren't!
jokes.setup: Moira has arranged beautiful boxwood trees around the motel and John is proudly presenting them.
jokes.punchline: David and Alexis both completely fail to appreciate the new plants — David thinks they were already there; Alexis thinks there were more before — confirming Moira's pre-emptive martyrdom was entirely justified.
jokes.explanation: The perfect payoff to Moira's martyr speech. David and Alexis don't just fail to appreciate it — they actively misremember the space as having MORE plants before. The escalation from 'weren't those here before?' to 'weren't there MORE?' is excellent structure and vindicates Moira completely, which is funnier than simple ingratitude.

jokes.text: They'll never see it. Throw it on the long list of things I've done for my children they don't appreciate.
jokes.setup: The children fail to appreciate or even notice the boxwoods Moira fought for.
jokes.punchline: Moira's aside — adding this to 'the long list' of unappreciated things — plays her genuine hurt for laughs while also being recognizably accurate.
jokes.explanation: A warm, recognizable parent-of-ungrateful-children beat. The implication that there is already a 'long list' adds depth without over-explaining. Character-specific and well-delivered.

jokes.text: Oh, there's the condom right there, David. — Ew! Eww!
jokes.setup: The family is dispersing; the earlier condom reference seemed forgotten.
jokes.punchline: Johnny spots and announces the condom's location to David — bringing back the gross detail as a scene-closing callback, met with appropriate disgust.
jokes.explanation: A functional callback that closes the episode on a gross, low-stakes joke. The callback structure is the main craft element — it brings back an earlier thread for a satisfying (if not brilliant) button. The kids' 'Ew! Eww!' is a good physical-vocal reaction beat.

tmdb_overview: Moira tries to beautify the town. David misuses the company credit card. A grooming misstep forces Alexis and Mutt to question their relationship.
insight: Moira's town council showdown generates 153 jokes across 57 minutes—deadpan delivery carries a middling score.
